refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (total time - unplanned downtime) / total time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold at the end of the evaluation window and does not fall below it during interim checkpoints.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall be capable of operating without unplanned interruption for the duration specified in the operational profile.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall recover from faults and return to operational readiness within the time constraints defined by the customer availability target.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant operational pathways with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity during component degradation.
DP2: Design preventive maintenance scheduling with condition-based monitoring to detect and replace components before failure, reducing unplanned downtime.
DP3: Architect modular subsystems with standardized interfaces and hot-swappable modules to enable rapid replacement without full system shutdown.
DP4: Establish operational resilience through graceful degradation modes that allow partial functionality when full capacity is unavailable.
DP5: Implement comprehensive fault detection and isolation mechanisms with automated alerting to enable rapid human intervention and recovery.
DP6: Design system architecture with geographic or functional distribution to reduce correlated failure modes and enable load redistribution.
DP7: Establish spare inventory and logistics protocols to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR) for critical failure modes.
DP8: Implement real-time health monitoring with predictive analytics to forecast degradation and trigger proactive maintenance actions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: Composite DP: Achieve availability ≥0.97 through integrated fault tolerance (DP1: redundancy + failover), condition-based maintenance (DP2: predictive monitoring), modular architecture (DP3: hot-swap capability), graceful degradation (DP4), rapid fault isolation (DP5), and optimized MTTR via spare logistics (DP7). DP6 (distribution) and DP8 (predictive analytics) are secondary enablers contingent on system topology and operational maturity.

potential_sources: DP1 (redundancy) and DP3 (modularity) are tightly coupled—redundant pathways must align with module boundaries to avoid cross-module dependencies that defeat isolation.
DP2 (preventive maintenance) and DP5 (fault detection) share sensor/monitoring infrastructure; poor interface design between them can create data silos and missed alerts.
DP4 (graceful degradation) and DP1 (failover) must be coordinated: failover logic must know which degraded modes are acceptable vs. requiring full recovery.
DP7 (spare logistics) depends on accurate MTTR estimates from DP5 (fault isolation); misalignment causes either excess inventory or insufficient spares.
DP8 (predictive analytics) introduces a new dependency on data quality and model accuracy; if decoupled from DP2 and DP5, it becomes a parallel system rather than an integrated capability.
coupling_check.notes: The composite DP is logically sound but requires explicit architectural boundaries to prevent subsystem coupling. Key risk: treating redundancy, maintenance, and monitoring as independent initiatives rather than co-designed elements. Recommend explicit interface contracts between fault detection (DP5) and maintenance scheduling (DP2), and between failover logic (DP1) and graceful degradation modes (DP4).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target threshold is stable and measurable over the agreed evaluation period; RISK: Threshold may shift mid-evaluation or measurement methodology may be ambiguous (e.g., does availability include planned maintenance windows?).

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not prohibit redundancy or automated failover; RISK: Safety-critical constraints may forbid unattended failover, forcing manual intervention and increasing MTTR.

ASSUMPTION: System has identifiable failure modes with known or estimable failure rates; RISK: Novel or latent failure modes (e.g., software bugs, environmental factors) may not be captured in availability models.
ASSUMPTION: Spare parts and logistics infrastructure can support the required MTTR; RISK: Supply chain delays, geographic isolation, or cost constraints may make DP7 infeasible.

ASSUMPTION: Monitoring and predictive analytics infrastructure is available and reliable; RISK: If monitoring itself fails or produces false positives, it can degrade availability rather than improve it.
ASSUMPTION: Modular architecture (DP3) is technically and economically feasible; RISK: Modularity may introduce interface overhead or performance penalties that offset availability gains.
ASSUMPTION: Graceful degradation (DP4) is acceptable to the customer; RISK: Some applications require full availability or nothing; partial functionality may be unacceptable.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine the FR to explicitly address: (1) Definition of 'operational availability'—does it include planned maintenance, partial functionality, or only full-capacity uptime? (2) Evaluation period duration and measurement methodology. (3) Constraints from safety, regulatory, or operational policies that may limit DP choices (e.g., no unattended failover, manual-only recovery). (4) Acceptable failure modes and graceful degradation thresholds. Revised FR: 'The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability ≥0.97 (measured as [uptime definition], excluding [planned maintenance/other exclusions]) over [evaluation period], subject to [safety/regulatory constraints], with acceptable graceful degradation modes defined as [list].' This will eliminate ambiguity and enable tighter DP-to-IM mapping.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated fault tolerance, availability management, predictive maintenance coordination, modular architecture governance, graceful degradation logic, fault isolation orchestration, and spare logistics optimization across the entire system.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests health, performance, and fault signals from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to feed condition-based monitoring (DP2) and rapid fault isolation (DP5).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Directs failover commands, hot-swap orchestration (DP3), and graceful degradation actions (DP4) to Vehicle and Teleoperation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Propagates critical fault isolation results and availability-critical decisions to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Supports redundancy coordination (DP1), distributed monitoring (DP6 secondary), and spare logistics communication (DP7).

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level orchestration layer designed to manage cross-cutting availability concerns. It naturally contains redundancy logic (DP1), failover coordination (DP1), predictive monitoring aggregation (DP2), modular architecture governance (DP3), graceful degradation policies (DP4), fault isolation algorithms (DP5), and spare logistics optimization (DP7). Minor diffusion: DP6 (distribution topology) and DP8 (predictive analytics maturity) may require coordination with Vehicle and Teleoperation for implementation details, but policy and orchestration remain in NGHE Solution.

Vehicle is a subsystem component, not a system-level orchestrator. While it implements redundancy at the vehicle level (DP1 partial), it cannot contain system-wide failover logic, cross-subsystem fault isolation (DP5), or spare logistics optimization (DP7). Graceful degradation (DP4) and predictive monitoring (DP2) would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Poor fit for composite DP.

Teleoperation is a subsystem for remote control and situational awareness. It cannot contain system-level availability orchestration (DP1, DP5, DP7). Condition-based maintenance (DP2) and graceful degradation (DP4) would require coordination across Vehicle and Safety Services. Modular architecture governance (DP3) is out of scope. Weak containment.

Safety Services is a specialized subsystem for safety-critical decisions. While it may consume fault isolation results (DP5) and graceful degradation alerts (DP4), it does not own redundancy (DP1), predictive monitoring (DP2), modular architecture (DP3), or spare logistics (DP7). It is a consumer of availability decisions, not the orchestrator. Poor fit.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The composite DP for integrated fault tolerance and availability management is fundamentally a system-level orchestration concern. NGHE Solution, as the top-level solution architecture, is the natural boundary for containing the policy, coordination logic, and cross-subsystem orchestration required by DP1–DP5 and DP7. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are subsystems that implement specific aspects of these DPs (e.g., Vehicle implements redundancy at the vehicle level; Safety Services consumes fault isolation alerts) but do not own the system-wide orchestration. Placing the composite DP in NGHE Solution ensures cohesion of availability governance and minimizes diffusion of critical fault tolerance logic across multiple subsystems.
containment_risks: DP6 (distribution topology) may require Vehicle and Teleoperation to expose their network topology and failover capabilities; NGHE Solution must define clear interfaces (Network Link) to avoid tight coupling.

DP8 (predictive analytics) is contingent on operational maturity; if analytics models are developed in isolation (e.g., in a separate analytics service), diffusion risk increases. Recommend that analytics remain a capability within NGHE Solution's monitoring subsystem.

DP2 (condition-based maintenance) requires real-time telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation; latency or data quality issues in the Telemetry interface could degrade predictive accuracy. Establish SLAs for telemetry ingestion.
DP7 (spare logistics optimization) may require integration with external supply chain systems; ensure Network Link interface is robust and that NGHE Solution does not become a bottleneck.

Graceful degradation (DP4) logic must be coordinated with Safety Services to ensure safety constraints are not violated during degradation transitions. Define clear handoff protocols via SafetyAlert interface.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: The composite DP is well-formulated and appropriately scoped to NGHE Solution. No revision to DP choice is necessary. However, recommend decomposing the composite DP into a hierarchy of sub-DPs within NGHE Solution to improve traceability and implementation clarity: (1) DP1.1: Redundancy policy engine, (1.2) Failover orchestration engine; (2.1) Telemetry aggregation, (2.2) Predictive model inference; (3.1) Module registry and hot-swap controller; (4.1) Degradation policy engine; (5.1) Fault isolation algorithm; (7.1) Spare logistics optimizer. This decomposition maintains NGHE Solution as the owning boundary while enabling modular implementation and testing within the solution.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability ≥0.97 is measurable and verifiable over the agreed evaluation period. NGHE Solution as the owning IM boundary provides a single point of orchestration for collecting telemetry, executing failover logic, and coordinating graceful degradation. Verification can be performed via instrumentation of the Telemetry interface (inbound health signals) and Tasking interface (outbound control actions). Safety constraints are propagated via SafetyAlert interface, enabling traceability to worksite safety plan.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR allocation to NGHE Solution is clear: the solution owns system-level availability orchestration (DP1–DP5, DP7) and coordinates subsystem implementations (Vehicle redundancy, Teleoperation failover capability, Safety Services constraints). Subsystem responsibilities are well-defined: Vehicle and Teleoperation implement redundancy and failover capabilities at their level; Safety Services validates graceful degradation against safety constraints. Network Link interface supports cross-subsystem coordination without tight coupling.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The composite DP (integrated fault tolerance, predictive monitoring, modular architecture, graceful degradation, rapid fault isolation, spare logistics optimization) directly addresses the FR requirement to achieve and maintain availability ≥0.97. Each DP element is necessary: DP1 (redundancy/failover) and DP7 (spare logistics) reduce downtime; DP2 (predictive monitoring) prevents faults; DP3 (hot-swap) enables rapid recovery; DP4 (graceful degradation) maintains partial availability during faults; DP5 (fault isolation) accelerates MTTR. The composite structure is justified by the interdependencies among these mechanisms. DP6 and DP8 are correctly marked as secondary enablers contingent on topology and maturity, avoiding over-specification.

diffusion_risks: DP2 (condition-based maintenance) depends on real-time telemetry quality and latency from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. If telemetry ingestion SLAs are not enforced, predictive accuracy degrades and availability target may not be met. Recommend explicit SLA definition for Telemetry interface (e.g., max latency, data completeness).

DP8 (predictive analytics) is contingent on operational maturity. If analytics models are developed or trained outside NGHE Solution (e.g., in a separate data science service), model versioning, deployment, and validation logic may diffuse. Recommend that model inference and policy updates remain within NGHE Solution's monitoring subsystem to maintain cohesion.

DP4 (graceful degradation) logic must coordinate with Safety Services to ensure safety constraints are not violated during degradation transitions. If handoff protocols are not clearly defined, safety-critical decisions may be delayed or contradicted. Recommend explicit state machine and handoff rules via SafetyAlert interface.

DP6 (distribution topology) requires Vehicle and Teleoperation to expose network topology and failover capabilities. If these subsystems do not provide clear, stable interfaces for topology discovery, NGHE Solution may become tightly coupled to subsystem internals. Recommend that Network Link interface includes a topology registry or discovery protocol.

DP7 (spare logistics optimization) may require integration with external supply chain systems. If Network Link interface is not robust or if NGHE Solution becomes a bottleneck for logistics queries, availability gains from spare optimization may be negated by communication delays. Recommend asynchronous messaging and caching strategies.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. The high containment is justified: NGHE Solution is the system-level orchestration layer and naturally owns policy, coordination logic, and cross-subsystem orchestration for availability. Vehicle (0.45 containment) and Teleoperation (0.4 containment) are subsystems that implement specific aspects (e.g., vehicle-level redundancy, remote failover capability) but do not own system-wide orchestration. Safety Services (0.35 containment) is a consumer of availability decisions, not the orchestrator. Minor diffusion in DP6 and DP8 is acceptable and expected: DP6 requires subsystem topology exposure, and DP8 requires operational data; both are managed via well-defined interfaces (Network Link, Telemetry) rather than scattered ownership. Recommend decomposing the composite DP into a hierarchy of sub-DPs within NGHE Solution (DP1.1, DP1.2, DP2.1, DP2.2, DP3.1, DP4.1, DP5.1, DP7.1) to improve traceability and enable modular implementation without changing the owning boundary.

recommended_revisions: Decompose the composite DP into a sub-DP hierarchy within NGHE Solution: (DP1.1) Redundancy policy engine, (DP1.2) Failover orchestration engine, (DP2.1) Telemetry aggregation, (DP2.2) Predictive model inference, (DP3.1) Module registry and hot-swap controller, (DP4.1) Degradation policy engine, (DP5.1) Fault isolation algorithm, (DP7.1) Spare logistics optimizer. This maintains NGHE Solution as the owning boundary while enabling modular implementation and testing.

Define explicit SLAs for the Telemetry interface: maximum latency (e.g., <100ms), data completeness (e.g., >99%), and error rates. Establish monitoring and alerting to detect telemetry degradation and trigger fallback strategies (e.g., conservative failover thresholds).

Establish a model governance process for DP8 (predictive analytics): define model versioning, validation, deployment, and rollback procedures. Ensure that model inference and policy updates remain within NGHE Solution's monitoring subsystem to avoid diffusion.

Define explicit state machine and handoff rules for DP4 (graceful degradation) coordination with Safety Services. Document the SafetyAlert interface protocol, including message types, timing constraints, and conflict resolution strategies.

Specify the Network Link interface protocol for DP6 (distribution topology) and DP7 (spare logistics): include topology discovery/registry, failover capability advertisement, and asynchronous logistics messaging. Avoid tight coupling by using publish-subscribe or event-driven patterns.

Establish acceptance test criteria for each sub-DP: (DP1.1/DP1.2) Failover latency <5s, (DP2.1/DP2.2) Fault detection latency <10s, (DP3.1) Hot-swap downtime <1s, (DP4.1) Graceful degradation without safety violations, (DP5.1) Fault isolation accuracy >95%, (DP7.1) Spare availability >90%. Verify these criteria during evaluation period to confirm availability ≥0.97.

Document the allocation of DP responsibilities to subsystems: Vehicle implements redundancy at vehicle level and exposes failover capability via Tasking interface; Teleoperation implements remote failover capability and exposes health signals via Telemetry interface; Safety Services validates graceful degradation and propagates safety constraints via SafetyAlert interface. Ensure subsystem design reviews confirm these allocations.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR-DP-IM alignment and feasibility at the current level (all three gates pass). However, the recommended_revisions explicitly call for decomposition of the composite DP into a sub-DP hierarchy (DP1.1, DP1.2, DP2.1, DP2.2, DP3.1, DP4.1, DP5.1, DP7.1) to improve traceability, enable modular implementation, and manage diffusion risks. The containment_notes indicate that NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment with acceptable 0.08 diffusion, but decomposition will strengthen architectural clarity and reduce implementation risk. Given high safety criticality, explicit sub-level decomposition is warranted to ensure each mechanism (redundancy, predictive monitoring, hot-swap, graceful degradation, fault isolation, spare logistics) is independently verifiable and traceable to safety constraints. Proceeding to next-level sub-FRs will operationalize the recommended decomposition and establish clear ownership and acceptance criteria for each sub-mechanism.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Enforce redundancy policy (active-active or active-passive) to ensure failover readiness and minimize failover latency to <5s.
FR1.2: Orchestrate failover transitions across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems with coordinated state management and safety constraint validation.
FR2.1: Aggregate real-time telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems with latency <100ms and data completeness >99%.
FR2.2: Infer system health state and predict faults using telemetry data with detection latency <10s and accuracy sufficient to prevent availability degradation.
FR3.1: Maintain module registry and execute hot-swap operations (module replacement, restart, re-integration) with downtime <1s and zero data loss.
FR4.1: Execute graceful degradation transitions (mode reduction, capability shedding) while maintaining safety constraints and coordinating with Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface.
FR5.1: Isolate faults to root cause (component, subsystem, network link) with accuracy >95% to accelerate MTTR and inform spare logistics decisions.
FR7.1: Optimize spare parts inventory and logistics coordination to ensure spare availability >90% and minimize lead time impact on system availability.

revise.why: The composite DP structure is logically sound and passes the FR-DP gate, but decomposition into sub-DPs (DP1.1–DP7.1) is necessary to operationalize the recommended revisions and establish clear implementation boundaries within NGHE Solution. Sub-DP decomposition will enable: (1) modular implementation and testing of each mechanism independently; (2) explicit acceptance criteria and verification strategies for each sub-mechanism (e.g., failover latency <5s, fault detection latency <10s); (3) traceability from sub-FRs to sub-DPs to implementation modules (e.g., Redundancy Policy Engine, Failover Orchestration Engine, Telemetry Aggregation Service); (4) risk management for diffusion factors (e.g., telemetry SLAs, model governance, graceful degradation handoff rules, topology discovery protocol, spare logistics messaging). This decomposition maintains NGHE Solution as the owning IM boundary while improving architectural clarity and safety assurance.

assumptions: NGHE Solution is the system-level orchestration layer and owns availability policy, coordination logic, and cross-subsystem orchestration.
Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems implement redundancy and failover capabilities at their level and expose these capabilities via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link).
Safety Services is a consumer of availability decisions and validates graceful degradation against safety constraints via SafetyAlert interface.
Telemetry interface SLAs (latency <100ms, completeness >99%) are achievable and will be enforced by Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems.
Predictive analytics models (DP8) will be developed, validated, and deployed within NGHE Solution's monitoring subsystem to maintain cohesion and avoid diffusion.
Graceful degradation logic can be coordinated with Safety Services via explicit state machine and handoff rules without introducing safety-critical delays.
Network Link interface will support topology discovery/registry and asynchronous logistics messaging without becoming a bottleneck.
Spare logistics optimization can be integrated with external supply chain systems via asynchronous messaging and caching strategies.
open_risks: Telemetry quality and latency from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems may degrade under high-load or adverse network conditions, reducing predictive accuracy and availability target achievement. Mitigation: Define explicit SLAs, establish monitoring and alerting, implement fallback strategies (e.g., conservative failover thresholds).

Model governance for predictive analytics (DP8) may diffuse if model development, validation, and deployment are not centralized within NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Establish model governance process with versioning, validation, deployment, and rollback procedures.

Graceful degradation transitions may violate safety constraints if handoff protocols with Safety Services are not clearly defined. Mitigation: Define explicit state machine and handoff rules via SafetyAlert interface with timing constraints and conflict resolution strategies.

Network Link interface may become tightly coupled to subsystem internals if topology discovery and failover capability advertisement are not standardized. Mitigation: Specify topology registry/discovery protocol and use publish-subscribe or event-driven patterns.

Spare logistics optimization may be negated by communication delays if Network Link interface is not robust or if NGHE Solution becomes a bottleneck. Mitigation: Implement asynchronous messaging and caching strategies.

Sub-DP implementation may introduce new diffusion risks if module boundaries are not clearly defined or if responsibilities are not clearly allocated to implementation teams. Mitigation: Document allocation of sub-DP responsibilities to implementation modules and establish design review checkpoints.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface SLA compliance metrics: latency distribution (p50, p95, p99), data completeness rate, error rate. Target: latency <100ms (p99), completeness >99%, error rate <0.1%.
Failover orchestration latency measurements: time from fault detection to failover completion. Target: <5s.
Fault detection latency measurements: time from fault occurrence to detection by predictive model. Target: <10s.
Hot-swap operation downtime measurements: time from module removal to re-integration. Target: <1s, zero data loss.
Graceful degradation validation: confirmation that degradation transitions do not violate safety constraints and that SafetyAlert handoff rules are followed.
Fault isolation accuracy metrics: percentage of faults correctly isolated to root cause. Target: >95%.
Spare availability metrics: percentage of time spare parts are available when needed. Target: >90%.
System-level availability measurements: uptime percentage over agreed evaluation period. Target: ≥0.97.
Model governance documentation: versioning, validation, deployment, and rollback procedures for predictive analytics models.
Network Link interface protocol specification: topology discovery/registry, failover capability advertisement, asynchronous logistics messaging.
SafetyAlert interface protocol specification: message types, timing constraints, conflict resolution strategies.
Sub-DP to implementation module mapping: traceability from sub-DPs to code modules, services, or components.
